metadata.summary: Enables LLMs to call external functions, APIs, and code via tool registry with request-execute-respond loop and iteration cap.

content.title: Tool Use Basics
content.summary: Enables LLMs to call external functions, APIs, and code by defining a tool registry (name → callable), converting it to provider-specific JSON schema, and running a request-execute-respond loop with a hard iteration cap. Covers OpenAI function calling and Anthropic tool use patterns.

why.p: LLMs hallucinate when performing real-time lookups, precise calculations, or stateful operations. Tool use grounds responses in authoritative external data and enables autonomous multi-step workflows. Description quality is the #1 reliability lever — vague descriptions cause wrong tool selection even with correct implementations.

list.item: Accessing real-time or external data (weather, stocks, database records, web search)
Precise computation required (math, date arithmetic, aggregation) where LLMs hallucinate
Autonomous agents taking actions (file writes, API calls, messages) in multi-step workflows
ReAct-style (Reason + Act) loops where the model iterates until a goal is achieved
Grounding LLM responses in authoritative data sources rather than training knowledge
Task solvable with a single in-context prompt — tool calling adds latency and complexity
All required information is already in the prompt context
Destructive tools (delete, send emails, charge money) with no human approval gate
Small/fast models (Haiku, gpt-4o-mini) with complex schemas (>5 tools, nested params) — selection accuracy degrades

rule.statement: Cap every tool use loop at a fixed iteration limit (10 is a safe default) and return a partial result with a [max_iterations_reached] marker when the limit is hit — never run unbounded.
rule.rationale: If tool results never satisfy the model's reasoning, it keeps calling tools until the context overflows or costs spike. A hard cap is mandatory for production stability. Infinite loops: if tool results never satisfy the model's reasoning, it keeps calling tools until the context overflows; always cap iterations (10 is a safe default).

example.title: Iteration-capped tool loop for OpenAI
example.note: Wrap json.loads in try/except — the model occasionally produces malformed JSON arguments, especially with complex schemas. json.loads(tool_call.function.arguments) raises JSONDecodeError when the model generates malformed JSON (rare but happens with complex schemas); always catch and return a tool error instead of crashing the loop.

rule.statement: Never assume OpenAI and Anthropic tool schemas are interchangeable; maintain a single tool registry and translate to provider-specific format at call time.
rule.rationale: OpenAI uses "type": "function" wrapper + "parameters"; Anthropic uses "input_schema" not "parameters"; Gemini uses protos.FunctionDeclaration. A single registry must translate between formats. Provider schema differences: OpenAI uses "type": "function" wrapper, Anthropic uses "input_schema" not "parameters", Gemini uses protos.FunctionDeclaration — a single tool registry must translate between formats.

example.title: Hardcoding Anthropic schema into OpenAI call
example.code: # Hardcoding Anthropic schema into OpenAI call tools = [{"name": "get_weather", "input_schema": {...}}] # wrong for OpenAI client.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o", tools=tools)

rule.statement: Always include the tool_call_id (OpenAI) or tool_use_id (Anthropic) in the tool result message; omitting it causes a validation error.
rule.rationale: Both providers require correlation IDs in tool results to match calls to responses. Missing IDs fail silently or raise SDK errors depending on the provider version. Missing tool_call_id in the tool result message causes a validation error in OpenAI SDK; Anthropic uses tool_use_id — providers differ, test both paths.

section.name: Tool Definition Quality and Description Quality

rule.statement: Write tool descriptions as if explaining to a junior developer who does not know the system — the model uses the description, not the code, to decide when to call a tool.
rule.rationale: Vague descriptions are the #1 cause of wrong tool selection. A description that says "get data" is worse than one that says "fetch current weather conditions for a city by calling the external weather API". Vague tool descriptions cause the model to select the wrong tool or skip tool use entirely; description quality is the #1 reliability lever.

rule.statement: Return structured error objects from tools ({"error": "reason"}) instead of raising exceptions; let the model decide how to recover.
rule.rationale: Exceptions propagate out of the tool loop and crash the agent. A structured error lets the model reason about failure and potentially retry with different arguments.

rule.statement: Sandbox any code execution tool (subprocess, exec) to prevent model-generated code from accessing the host filesystem or network without restriction.
rule.rationale: A model-generated os.system("rm -rf /") in an unsandboxed subprocess is a critical security incident. Code execution tools must be isolated. Code execution tools (running arbitrary Python) must be sandboxed; a model-generated os.system("rm -rf /") call in an unsandboxed subprocess is a critical security incident.

rule.statement: Require explicit human confirmation before executing any tool that modifies external state (database writes, API calls that create/delete resources, email sending).
rule.rationale: Autonomous tool execution without approval is appropriate only for read-only tools. Destructive actions triggered by ambiguous natural language intent cause irreversible harm. Human checkpoint required before any tool that modifies external state (database writes, API calls that create/delete resources, email sending) — autonomous tool execution without approval is appropriate only for read-only tools.

antipattern.description: Passing large tool results (>50KB) back to the model without truncation.
antipattern.reason: Very large tool results inflate context dramatically, pushing other messages out of the window and causing the model to lose track of earlier instructions. Tool result size: very large tool results (>50KB) sent back to the model inflate context dramatically; truncate or summarize large results before returning them.

antipattern.description: Executing parallel tool calls (OpenAI default) when tools share mutable state.
antipattern.reason: Parallel calls can produce race conditions — e.g., two concurrent writes to the same database row. Use sequential execution for stateful tools. Parallel tool calls (OpenAI default) can cause race conditions if tools share state; use tool_choice="auto" carefully with stateful tools.

rule.statement: Execute independent tool calls in parallel using asyncio.gather; log each tool call with name, args, result, and latency to a persistent trace.
rule.rationale: Sequential execution of N independent tools multiplies latency by N. Parallel execution with async implementations reduces multi-tool workflows to the slowest single call. Trace logs enable replay on failure. Execute independent tool calls in parallel (use asyncio.gather with async tool implementations) to reduce latency in multi-step pipelines.

section.name: Agentic Workflow and Integration
section.note: A subagent implementing tool use should define a tool registry (name → callable), convert the registry to provider-specific schema, run the request-execute-respond loop with an iteration cap, and return a structured summary of tools called and their results. For autonomous agents, the subagent should log every tool call (name, args, result, latency) to a persistent trace so errors can be replayed. Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (scaffold tool registry, schema generator, and execution loop as SDD tasks), General-purpose subagent (act as the "brain" in a tool-use loop; delegate actual tool execution to specialized subagents).

example.title: Tool use system prompt
example.code: You have access to these tools: {tool_names}. Use them to answer the user's request. Call tools step by step. After each tool result, reason about whether the goal is achieved. Stop after at most {max_iterations} tool calls. User request: {request}

example.title: Schema generation prompt
example.code: Generate an OpenAI function calling schema for this Python function: {function_source} Include: name, description from docstring, parameter types from type hints, required fields. Output: valid JSON matching the OpenAI tool schema format.

section.name: CLI Tools and Libraries
section.text: openai (Python SDK) — OpenAI function calling (pip install openai / https://pypi.org/project/openai/) anthropic (Python SDK) — Anthropic tool use (pip install anthropic / https://pypi.org/project/anthropic/) google-generativeai — Gemini function calling (pip install google-generativeai / https://pypi.org/project/google-generativeai/) langchain-core — Tool abstraction layer for multi-provider (pip install langchain-core / https://pypi.org/project/langchain-core/)

section.name: Services and Platforms
section.text: OpenAI Function Calling (SaaS, Yes) — Built into Chat Completions; supports parallel calls; max 128 tools Anthropic Tool Use (SaaS, Yes) — stop_reason == "tool_use" pattern; max 64 tools Gemini Function Calling (SaaS, Yes) — protos.Tool schema; supports parallel; max 64 tools LangChain Tools (OSS, Yes) — 100+ pre-built tools (search, math, file, SQL); provider-agnostic Composio (SaaS, Yes) — Managed tool integrations (GitHub, Slack, Gmail) with OAuth; reduces boilerplate

list.item: Write tool descriptions as if explaining to a junior developer who does not know the system
Return structured error objects from tools instead of raising exceptions
Execute independent tool calls in parallel using asyncio.gather
Set a hard iteration cap in every tool loop and return a partial result with [max_iterations_reached]
Version tool schemas; when a tool's parameter schema changes, old conversations will fail
Test schema translation between OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini formats thoroughly

template.purpose: Iteration-capped tool loop for OpenAI (≤35 lines)
JSON schema template for a single tool definition
